preFix.title: Spinning Manx Loaghtan wool
preFix.subtitle: A primitive rare-breed fleece spun woollen for a durable, naturally coloured DK-weight yarn
preFix.excerpt: Manx Loaghtan is a primitive horned breed from the Isle of Man with a naturally dark brown fleece and a short, variable staple. The fibre contains some kemp in lower-grade fleeces and is not next-to-skin soft, but its warm brown colour and exceptional durability make it ideal for outerwear and hard-wearing accessories.
preFix.sourceNotes: Manx Loaghtan breed characteristics are documented by the Rare Breeds Survival Trust and the Manx Loaghtan Sheep Breeders Group. Spinning behaviour is observation-led from guild notes.

content.text: originating on the Isle of Man, now kept in small conservation flocks across the British Isles. The fleece is naturally

: a warm brown that needs no dyeing. Staple length is short to medium (6 to 10 cm), micron count typically 30 to 36, and the character is springy and resilient rather than soft. The challenge of Loaghtan is managing

: coarse hollow fibres that appear in varying quantities depending on the individual animal and the care taken during fleece selection. A Loaghtan fleece with minimal kemp spins into a durable yarn for hats, bags, and outerwear; a heavily kempy fleece is better used for rug-making or blending rather than garment spinning.

attrs.body: Keep loose hair, scarves, and cuffs away from the drive band and flyer. The flyer spins fast and catches fabric instantly.
attrs.title: Wheel safety

items.name: Manx Loaghtan raw locks or carded roving, 100 g
Spinning wheel, castle or Saxony style
Hand cards for carding raw locks (optional)
Measuring tape for checking wraps per inch

content.text: Before carding, separate the locks and examine each one against a light source. Kemp fibres are visible as shorter, bright white or pale strands mixed in with the darker brown wool fibre. A lock with occasional kemp is acceptable; a lock where kemp makes up more than 20 per cent of the visible fibre is best set aside for rug-making. Running a lock between finger and thumb will also reveal kemp: the coarser fibres prickle noticeably even before spinning.

Card the washed locks on hand cards into rolags. Loaghtan's short staple suits a woollen preparation; worsted combing is difficult with staple under 8 cm. Card lightly rather than aggressively: over-carding breaks the short fibres and produces a fluffy, weak rolag. The resulting rolag should be springy and hold together when rolled off the card.

Set the wheel to a ratio of 8:1 to 10:1 for a DK-weight yarn. Loaghtan's short staple requires more twist than longer-staple breeds to hold together; spinning too slowly at a low ratio produces weak, under-twisted singles that break under plying. Use a long draw, releasing the rolag and allowing twist to run up into the drafting zone. The short fibres lock together when twist enters before the rolag is over-drafted. Aim for approximately 18 to 22 wraps per 2.5 cm for a DK-weight single.

Ply two singles together at a ratio of 5:1 to 6:1. Loaghtan holds ply twist well and the 2-ply yarn is noticeably stronger than the singles alone. Wind the skein on the niddy-noddy, tie loosely at four points, and soak in warm water for 15 minutes. Snap once to set the twist and hang to dry without weight, preserving the loft of the woollen-spun fibre. Any remaining kemp fibres become visible in the finished skein; this is a feature of genuine primitive-breed yarn rather than a defect.

items.fix: Increase the wheel ratio to insert more twist per treadle stroke. Shorten the long draw: release smaller sections of rolag so the twist can catch the short fibres before they drift free.
items.cause: Too little twist for the short staple, or the long draw released too much fibre at once
items.problem: The singles break repeatedly during spinning

items.fix: Sort more carefully at the lock stage before carding, separating out the kempy locks for rug or craft use. For future purchases, ask for fleece from young animals (two-shear and under) which tend to have less kemp.
items.cause: The fleece had a higher-than-average kemp content, or the fibre came from an older animal
items.problem: The finished yarn feels much coarser than expected
